retrieved_docs.title: Wrist Imaging -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The osseous anatomy of the wrist includes the distal radius and ulna, eight carpal bones, and the five metacarpals (Figure 1). The complexity of this three arc system creates multiple synergistic joints that allow the wrist a dynamic range of motion. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Radiocarpal Joint -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The wrist is a joint complex comprised of three other joints: the radiocarpal joint, the ulnocarpal joint, and the distal radioulnar joint (DRUJ). The radiocarpal joint is a synovial joint formed by the articulation between the distal radius and the scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum as well as the soft tissue structures that hold the joint together.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Structure and Function -- Wrist bones, metacarpals, and fingers
retrieved_docs.content: The 8 carpal bones are divided into proximal and distal rows. The proximal wrist bones articulate with the radius. The proximal row includes the scaphoid, which resembles the prow of a ship and articulates with the trapezium distally. The trapezium then connects to the 1st metacarpal bone that supports the thumb. Moving from lateral to medial, the proximal row continues with the lunate (resembling the moon), triquetrum (which has 3 corners), and the rounded pisiform. The pisiform can be palpated on the hand's anterior aspect. This bone moves with hand motion, confirming its location within the wrist rather than the forearm.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Radiocarpal Joint -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The osseous structures of the radiocarpal joint include the distal radius, the scaphoid, the lunate, and the triquetrum.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Bones -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Boutonniere Deformity: The MCP (metacarpophalangeal) joints and the DIP (distal interphalangeal) joints are affected.  The primary damage is to flexion of the proximal interphalangeal joints due to damage to the central portion of the extensor hood. The boutonnière deformity is usually caused by rheumatoid arthritis. The term boutonnière refers to a buttonhole such as one might place a flower during a ceremony such as marriage.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Radiocarpal Joint -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The palmar radiocarpal ligament, a key instrument in joint stability, appears during week eight, preceding the dorsal radiocarpal ligament which appears during week ten. [12] Along with the ligaments of the radiocarpal joint, the formation of the triangular fibrocartilage disc is significant due to its articulation with radius and triquetrum which do not come in direct contact with one another. The triangular fibrocartilage disc is present by the end of week eight but is not well defined until week 14. [12] Encompassing the entire joint surface, including the ligaments and fibrocartilaginous structures, is the articular capsule that can be clearly defined by week eleven. The synovial membrane, the inner portion of the joint capsule develops later, and its intricate structure is identifiable by week 13. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Forearm Radius -- Structure and Function -- Joints and Ligaments:
retrieved_docs.content: The distal radius is attached to the lunate bone via two ligaments, the long and short radiolunate ligaments. These ligaments prevent hyperextension of the lunate bone during wrist extension. The distal radius is attached to the scaphoid via the radial collateral ligament of the wrist, and the radioscaphocapitate ligament attaches the volar aspect of the radius to the scaphoid and capitate carpal bones. Lastly, the dorsal radiotriquetral ligament is a wide ligament, which connects the dorsal radius to the dorsal scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Radiocarpal Joint -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The dorsal radiocarpal ligament originates on the dorsal surface of the radius spreads medially distally until it inserts on the lunate and triquetrum. [3] [4] The role of this ligament is to provide dorsal stabilization to the scaphoid throughout all wrist movements. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Wrist Joint -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The joint itself is formed through the articulations between the distal radius and the scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum. The proximal articulation forms a concave shape composed of a combination between the distal end of the radius and articular disk. The distal articulation is convex and composed of the scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum bones of the proximal hand. Note that the ulna is not part of the wrist joint itself, as it articulates with the distal via the distal radioulnar joint (DRUJ). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Wrist Arthrodesis -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The wrist joint is represented by the distal radioulnar joint, the radiocarpal joint, and the intercarpal joint.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Bones -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The wrist connects to the hand at the carpometacarpal (CMC) joints. [3] The first CMC forms from the articulation between the trapezium and the base of the first metacarpal. The base of the second metacarpal articulates with trapezium, trapezoid, and capitate. The base of the third metacarpal articulates with capitate. The base of the fourth metacarpal articulates with capitate and hamate. The fifth metacarpal's base articulates with the hamate. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Wrist Imaging -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: An intricate array of intrinsic (carpal to carpal) and extrinsic (radius and ulna to carpal) ligaments binds the osseous structures and allows for compound motions. Each bone has specific connections to adjacent osseous structures that provide the overall balance of stability and movement for the wrist. Typical nomenclature of the ligaments describes the structures connected and the direction of their attachment either proximally to distally or from the radial to ulnar aspect. For example, the scapholunate ligament attaches the scaphoid with the lunate, referencing the specific carpals spanned from radial to ulnar direction. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Bones -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Ulnar Drift of the Hand: most commonly occurs in rheumatoid arthritis. The deviation of the hand toward the ulnar side occurs at the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints due to the bowing of the ligaments of the extensor digitorum.

retrieved_docs.title: Wrist Imaging -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The articular surface of osseous structures is covered with articular (hyaline) cartilage, a connective tissue composed of a dense network of collagen fibers resulting in tissue with tensile strength yet more flexible than bone. Together with synovial joint fluid, the hyaline cartilage provides a low friction surface for movement across joints and aids in shock absorption. [8] The various articulations of the osseous structures of the wrist, which include long bones, carpals, and metacarpals, provide a wide range of motion, including various combinations of flexion, extension, ulnar, and radial deviation, supination, and pronation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Radiocarpal Joint -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The soft tissue structures of the wrist are mainly comprised of the triangular fibrocartilage complex and the intrinsic and extrinsic ligaments. The ligaments of importance regarding the radiocarpal joint are the extrinsic ligaments which serve as a connection between the distal radius and the carpal bones. [3] The extrinsic ligaments of concern are the dorsal radiocarpal ligament, the short radiolunate ligament, the long radiolunate ligament, the radioscaphocapitate ligament, and the radioscapholunate ligament.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Radiocarpal Joint -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The distal radius has two concaved surfaces involved in the radiocarpal joint, the scaphoid fossa and the lunate fossa. These concavities provide a surface for direct articulation between the radius and the two carpal bones. [1] The articulation of the distal radius with the triquetrum is indirect and facilitated via a biconcave fibrocartilage expansion called the triangular fibrocartilage (TFC) disc proper. [2] The combination of the concaved fossae and the biconcave TFC allows for fluid articulation with the convex surfaces of the scaphoid and lunate and triquetrum. While most bones in the body require muscle contraction for motion, the scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum form an intercalated segment, and are purely dependent on surrounding mechanical forces for motion due to the fact they have no tendinous insertions. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1915
retrieved_docs.content: The division of joint capsule is an anatomical concept that encompasses specific subdivisions of the joint capsule in different regions of the upper extremity. In particular, the division of joint capsule of hand represents the hand‑specific portion of this structure, while the division of joint capsule of wrist denotes the wrist‑specific portion. Both the division of joint capsule of hand and the division of joint capsule of wrist are recognized as distinct subclasses of the broader division of joint capsule, reflecting the specialized anatomy of the joint capsule in these adjacent but functionally separate joints. Understanding these regional divisions aids clinicians in accurately describing joint anatomy, diagnosing joint‑related pathologies, and planning targeted interventions for the hand and wrist.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 236
retrieved_docs.content: bursal operation. Overall, this taxonomy clarifies how individual operative and diagnostic interventions on joints, hand, wrist, tendons, tendon sheaths, ligaments, cartilage, and bursae are nested within broader procedural categories, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate therapeutic or diagnostic codes and planning patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: injury (إصابة العضلات والعظام) as a child, and may also encompass congenital anomalies (مُتَلاَزِمَةٌ هَرْسِيَّة) as a child concept. Wrist injury is classified both as fractured wrist bone and as a closed distal forearm fracture, reinforcing the hierarchical relationship between wrist, hand, and general limb fracture classifications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1914
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration of fascia of hand is a medical procedure performed on the musculoskeletal system, specifically targeting the fascial tissue of the hand for removal of fluid or other material. This intervention is classified as a procedure on the musculoskeletal system, reflecting its role in diagnosing or treating conditions affecting hand fascia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as a pathology that can be applied to both بيلة سِّيسْتينِية and the radius, indicating that this qualifier is relevant to the disease entity بيلة سِّيسْتينِية as well as to the anatomical structure of the radius bone. This relationship highlights that the same pathological descriptor may be used across different medical contexts, encompassing both a specific disease and a skeletal element.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: The radius is identified as an allowed qualifier in several specialized domains, indicating that it can be the focus of detailed study or intervention in each area. In ultrastructural research, the radius is an allowed qualifier, meaning that subcellular imaging and analysis are applicable to this bone. Anatomical and histological investigations also list the radius as an allowed qualifier, reflecting its significance in the study of bone structure and tissue composition. Cytological assessment includes the radius as an allowed qualifier, allowing cellular‑level examination of bone cells. In the context of transplantation, the radius is an allowed qualifier, signifying that it may be considered for grafting or transplant procedures. Clinically, the radius is the direct_procedure_site_of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna, highlighting its central role in orthopedic care and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: The skeletal x-ray of the shoulder and upper arm is an imaging study that directly pertains to the arm, providing visualization of the bony structures of both the shoulder region and the upper portion of the arm.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified as other non‑traumatic joint disorders and its primary finding site is the joints (klouby). Other non‑traumatic joint disorders are linked to the concept of اعتلال مفصلي (joint disease). In the context of surgical procedures, Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage is a subcategory (has parent) of Incision and excision of joint structures and it classifies the anatomical category “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage.” Incision and excision of joint structures also has a relationship with the joints (klouby). This integrated knowledge connects endocrine‑related arthropathy to non‑traumatic joint pathology, emphasizes its joint involvement, and outlines the hierarchical relationship between joint‑focused surgical interventions and the specific joint structures they address.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 0 -- Community 9
retrieved_docs.content: of phalanx of toe, Excision of lesion of tarsal and metatarsal to استئصال آفة العظام, and the various sequestrectomy procedures to their specific sequestrum‑excision counterparts, reinforcing the shared classification of bone‑lesion removal and limited phalangeal resection. Joint pathology is represented by “Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder,” mapped to “other non‑traumatic joint disorders,” linked to the anatomical site klouby and the Arabic term اعتلال مفصلي (joint disease), highlighting metabolic‑related arthropathy within the non‑traumatic joint spectrum. Upper‑extremity and hand interventions are organized under “Hand, Wrist: Incision,” a child of both “Other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” and “OPERATIVE PROCEDURES ON THE UPPER EXTREMITY: WRIST AND HAND.” This node includes Division of joint capsule of hand, Division of joint capsule of wrist, Drainage of tendon of hand, Puncture of bursa of hand, Release for tenosynovitis of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 95
retrieved_docs.content: relationship, is linked to hand fracture (كسر اليد), which resides under both fractures of limbs (كسر في الأطراف) and fractures/dislocations of limbs (كسور وانخلاعات الأطراف). Wrist injury (إصابة في المعصم) is therefore synonymous with both عظم رسغ مكسور and the closed lower‑forearm fracture definition. Head injury trauma frequently co‑occurs with shoulder fracture (كسر الكتف). The generic unspecified injuries category (إصابات، غير مصنف في موضع آخر) also encompasses musculoskeletal injury, wound, and concomitant fracture (مُتَلاَزِمَةٌ هَرْسِيَّة), illustrating the hierarchical and associative structure that supports accurate classification, diagnosis, and coordinated patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified as a form of other non‑traumatic joint disorders and its clinical findings are located in the joints (klouby). The category of other non‑traumatic joint disorders is linked to the Arabic term اعتلال مفصلي, which denotes joint disease. Arthrotomy with removal of prosthesis is a surgical intervention that is performed directly on the joints (klouby) and is considered an inverse isa of بضع المفصل, indicating that it represents a specific type of joint removal procedure. The operative concept of Division of joint capsule encompasses two subtypes—Division of joint capsule of hand and Division of joint capsule of wrist—each defined as an isa relationship to the broader Division of joint capsule. This broader Division of joint capsule also has a relationship to Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage, a category that classifies procedures involving the division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage. Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage is a child (has parent) of Incision and excision of joint structures, and the Incision and excision of joint structures category itself is related to the joints (klouby). Together, these relationships integrate disease classification, anatomical localization, and surgical techniques relevant to joint pathology and its management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1047
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified as a form of other non‑traumatic joint disorders and its primary anatomic site is the joints (klouby). In the classification system, other non‑traumatic joint disorders are linked to the Arabic term اعتلال مفصلي, which also denotes joint disease. The anatomical concept of Division of joint capsule includes two specific subdivisions—Division of joint capsule of hand and Division of joint capsule of wrist—each identified as a type of Division of joint capsule. These subdivisions are further related to the broader category Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage, which serves to classify divisions of the joint capsule, ligaments, or cartilage. This broader category is a sub‑entity of the procedural concept Incision and excision of joint structures, indicating that surgical incision or excision may be performed on these joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage divisions. The procedure Incision and excision of joint structures is also associated with the joints (klouby). Collectively, these relationships illustrate how endocrine‑related arthropathy is grouped with non‑traumatic joint diseases, how joint capsule anatomy is organized into specific hand and wrist divisions, and how these anatomical divisions are connected to surgical interventions on the joints.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2652
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is applicable to multiple aspects of the radius, encompassing its anatomy and histology, its innervation, and its cytology, and it also pertains to the radius in the context of injury (zranění).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 274
retrieved_docs.content: Upper‑limb fractures (كسر الطرف العلوي) are organized under upper‑limb fracture categories and include distal forearm closed fractures; wrist bone fractures are children of hand fractures, and the inverse_isa relationship confirms this nesting. Injuries not classified elsewhere (إصابات، غير مصنف في موضع آخر) encompass musculoskeletal injury (إصابة العضلات والعظام), wound (جرح), and concomitant fracture (مُتَلاَزِمَةٌ هَرْسِيَّة). Shoulder fractures frequently co‑occur with head injury trauma. Recognizing these cause‑effect, co‑occurrence, and hierarchical relationships supports comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and coordinated management of patients with multiple traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy linked to endocrine or metabolic disorders is classified as an other non‑traumatic joint disorder affecting the joints (klouby) and is associated with the broader concept of joint disease (اعتلال مفصلي). In the musculoskeletal operative taxonomy, “Hand, Wrist: Incision” is positioned under “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” and under “OPERATIVE PROCEDURES ON THE UPPER EXTREMITY: WRIST AND HAND.” Its child procedures include Aspiration of bursa of hand (also classified within Injections and aspirations of muscles; tendons; bursa; joints and soft tissue), Aspiration of fascia of hand (a type of Procedure on musculoskeletal system), Division of joint capsule of hand, Division of joint capsule of wrist, Drainage of tendon of hand (an instance of Procedure on tendon), Puncture of bursa of hand (a type of Operation on bursa), Release for tenosynovitis of abductor pollicis longus, and Release for tenosynovitis of external pollicis brevis. Diagnostic procedure on

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1157
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Division of joint capsule” denotes anatomical subdivisions of the joint capsule and is further specified as the “Division of joint capsule of hand” and the “Division of joint capsule of wrist,” indicating that these are distinct categories within the broader concept. In addition, the “Division of joint capsule” has a relationship to the broader grouping “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage,” which functions as a classification framework that categorizes divisions of the joint capsule together with ligaments and cartilage. This framework, “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage,” classifies the “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage,” thereby linking the specific hand and wrist capsule divisions to a more comprehensive anatomical taxonomy that includes related connective tissues.
